coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive immunosensing of prostate specific antigen using poly cysteine caped by graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticle: A novel signal amplification strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Malekzad, Hediyeh
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Shadjou, Nasrin
Jouyban, Abolghasem
coredata.dc:description: A mediator-free electrochemical immunosensor for quantitation of prostate specific antigen (PSA) based on dual signal amplification strategy was fabricated. In this work, PSA-antibody (anti-PSA) was immobilized onto a green and biocompatible nanocomposite containing poly l-cysteine (P-Cys) as conductive matrix and graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/gold nanoparticles (GNPs) as dual signal amplification elements. Therefore, a novel multilayer film based on P-Cys, GQDs, and GNPs was exploited to develop a highly sensitive amperometric immunosensor for detection of PSA. Fully electrochemical methodology was used to prepare a new transducer on a gold surface which provided a high surface area to immobilize a high amount of the anti-PSA. Importantly, GNPs prepared by soft template synthesized method lead to compact morphology was achieved. The surface morphology of electrode surface was characterized by high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDX). Chemical compositions of the gold nanoparticles were analysed by an EDX. The immunosensor was employed for the detection of PSA in physiological pH. Under optimized condition the calibration curve for PSA concentration was linear up to 2–9pgmL−1 with lower limit of quantification of 1.8pgmL−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical immunosensor
Biomarker
Gold nanoparticles
Poly amino acid
Graphene quantum dot
Dual signal amplification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electropolymerization of l-Cys on the surface of Au
Electrodeposition of GQDs on the surface of P-Cys modified Au electrode
Electrodeposition of GNPs on the surface of P-Cys-GQDs modified Au electrode using soft template method
The SEM and EDX characterization of the modified sensing interface
Kinetic behaviors of the PSA immunosensors

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Health and Medical Education of Iran
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Health and Medical Education

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by the Ministry of Health and Medical Education of Iran (The Grant for Young assistant Professors), Tabriz University of Medical Sciences , for financial and instrumental supporting of this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of the label free electrochemical immunosensor for detection of prostate specific antigen with dual graphene quantum dot and gold nanoparticle signal amplification.

Electrochemical deposition of l-cysteine on the surface of Au electrode. The cyclic voltammogram depicts gradual growth of P-Cys after 5 successive cycles in the range of −1 to +1V with scan rate of 0.1V/s. Inset; CV of bare au electrode.
Schematic for electrodeposition of GQDs on P-Cys-Au electrode using 15 cycles in the potential range of 0–1V vs. Ag/AgCl with o.1V/s scan rate.
Chronoamperogram for the electrochemical deposition of GNPs on Au electrode. The pplied potential E=0V vs. Ag/AgCl and the record time was 600s.
SEM images of the sequential modified layers on Au electrode: A) P-Cys B); P-Cys/GQDs; C) P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs.

Cyclic voltamograms for four types of electrode modifications in the absence (a) and presence (b) of PSA antigen: a) Au/anti-PSA, b) Au/P-Cys/anti-PSA, c) Au/P-Cys/GQDs/anti-PSA and d) Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs/anti-PSA. Target solution containing 3mL PSA antigen to 10mL PBS, respectively.

A) CVs of the Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs electrode at different scan rates from 0.02 to 1V/s. B) Dependence of peak currents to the square root of scan rates for three prominent peaks of Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs electrode. C) The relationship between Napierian logarithm of peak currents (which is not defined for cathodic current peak with negative value) versus Neperian logarithm of scan rates. D) The relationship between peak potential versus Neperian logarithm of scan rates. E) Dependence of peak current versus scan rates.

A) Chronoamperogram of Au/PCys/GQDs/GNPs/anti-PSA for standard analysis of PSA with initial concentration of 3mL 30ngmL−1 PSA in 10mL PBS (E=−0.3, t=50s) and, the magnified illustration of the chronoamperogram. B) Linear plot for varying concentrations of standard PSA samples.
Comparison of the corresponding analytical parameters of the methods on determination of PSA.

row.entry: Label-free electrochemical immunoassay based on dual signal amplification strategy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different electrochemical methods for the detection of PSA.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive immunosensing of prostate specific antigen using poly cysteine caped by graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticle: A novel signal amplification strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanochemistry, Nano Technology Research Center, Urmia University, Urmia 57154, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nano Technology, Faculty of Science, Urmia University, Urmia 57154, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Kimia Idea Pardaz Azarbayjan (KIPA) Science Based Company, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Kimia Idea Pardaz Azarbayjan (KIPA) Science Based Company

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A mediator-free electrochemical immunosensor for quantitation of prostate specific antigen (PSA) based on dual signal amplification strategy was fabricated. In this work, PSA-antibody (anti-PSA) was immobilized onto a green and biocompatible nanocomposite containing poly l-cysteine (P-Cys) as conductive matrix and graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/gold nanoparticles (GNPs) as dual signal amplification elements. Therefore, a novel multilayer film based on P-Cys, GQDs, and GNPs was exploited to develop a highly sensitive amperometric immunosensor for detection of PSA. Fully electrochemical methodology was used to prepare a new transducer on a gold surface which provided a high surface area to immobilize a high amount of the anti-PSA. Importantly, GNPs prepared by soft template synthesized method lead to compact morphology was achieved. The surface morphology of electrode surface was characterized by high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDX). Chemical compositions of the gold nanoparticles were analysed by an EDX. The immunosensor was employed for the detection of PSA in physiological pH. Under optimized condition the calibration curve for PSA concentration was linear up to 2–9pgmL−1 with lower limit of quantification of 1.8pgmL−1.

ce:section.ce:para: Early diagnosis of cancer through screening the prominent cancer biomarkers in body fluids is the common and widely applied practice and the first step toward adopting efficient therapy, preventing metastasis and reducing mortality rate [1].

Prostate-specific antigen (PSA), which is a serine protease encoded by KLK3 gene, has been accepted as one of the major prostate cancer (PCa) indicators as blood-based biomarker due to its significant increase in the blood samples of patients with prostate cancer, up to 105 fold, and also benign prostate cases. The elevated level of blood PSA has also meaningful relation with the patient’s age, prostate size, inflammation, etc. Nevertheless, its level in serum have been monitored to diagnose the PCa, assess the cancer risk as well as the response to PCa therapy. It has been recommended that PSA screening should be followed by biopsy and evaluating urine biomarker for accurate diagnosis, however, since several incidents of increased PSA with negative biopsy reports have demonstrated PCa in later years, elevated PSA in blood, with threshold value of 4.0ngml−1, remains to be the serious warning factor in modern medical practice [2]. Moreover, the role of PSA levels in discriminating the stages of cancer, differentiating the localized and metastatic conditions, predicting the risk of recurrence for previously PCa-treated patients underline the implication of sensitive quantification of PSA in blood and serum by means of versatile, portable and highly sensitive devices in biomedical laboratories [3].

Recently, GQDs have been explored for their fascinating electrical and optical features and employed in variety of applications, such as photovoltaics, bioimaging, fuel cells and biosensing purposes. GQDs have distinct advantages over semiconductor inorganic QDs, being abundant due to their carbon base, lower toxicity, high solubility in various solvents and capability of being functionalized by attaching groups at their edges. In addition, due to their semi-molecular characteristics, they can be easily handled in contrast with colloidal semiconductor QDs [4]. Interestingly, GQDs having the nano-scaled graphene structure, exhibit both the properties of nanomaterials and desirable physical features of the other graphene-based structures, such as carboxylic acid functional groups and the π–π conjugated network [5,6]. The GQDs are usually in the size range of 3–20nm and have proven multivalent redox behavior in different sizes of 2.2±0.3, 2.6±0.2, and 3.0±0.3, which can be recorded by voltammetry techniques and utilized for electrochemical sensing approaches [7]. Besides numerous reports of optical biosensors based on GQDs, electrochemical GQDs-modified biosensors have been recently reported, as it has been demonstrated that GQDs possess superior electron donor and acceptor capabilities compared to microsized graphene sheet along with greater number of carboxyl groups and smaller dimensions compared to other carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene and carbon nanotubes [8,9]. Anisotropic gold nanostructures exhibit varying optical and electrocatalytical behaviors as a function of their shape and size. Up to now, several studies toward synthesis of shape-controlled nanostructures via electrochemical deposition techniques have been conducted and their excellent electrocatalytic features for sensing analytes have been concluded. The relation between size and morphology of various gold nanostructures, i.e. roughness, dominant metal facet and surface area toward reduction of oxygen and oxidation of methanol [10–12] have been evaluated, which suggests the possibility of imparting higher sensitivity to electrochemical biosensors by tailoring the morphology of nanostructures. Additionally, gold nanostructure-deposited electrode surfaces provide huge surface area with numerous sites for biomolecule immobilization. Li et al. [13] developed DNA biosensor modified with dendritic gold nanostructure and reported that the three-dimensional nanostructure considerably enhanced the hybridization of DNA with target strand with high sensitivity of femtomolar, upon immobilizing larger amount of probe DNA and reducing the repelling effect of the accessing target strand toward stabilized probe.

Label-free immunosensors with direct antigen recognition avoid time-consuming labeling protocol and offer simple assay with rapid detection and low assay cost in the current research, we have attempted to develop a sensitive electrochemical direct PSA assay and investigate the synergistic effect of GNPs and GQDs, as effective substrate and immobilization platform for anti-PSA, without using redox mediator.

Fabrication of nanoparticles (NPs) into polymer structure is a new method to enhance conductivity of polymer matrix and is an excellent pathway for preparation of polymers with high electrical properties which improved by incorporating NPs [13]. Lately, application of poly-amino acids modified by NPs and the use of these nanocomposites for bioanalytical aims has been described in the literature [14]. One of important amino acids for this purpose is l-Cysteine (l-Cys). According to sulfhydryl functional of l-Cys, it could be excellent candidate for electropolymerization and lead to preparation of a conductive interface with entitled “poly-cysteine”. On the other hand, this polymeric matrix could be functionalized by NPs for better electrochemical performance.

In this work, considering benefits of the GQDs, GNPs, and poly-cysteine (P-Cys), we used these materials for the construction of a novel immunosensor to apply the synergy contributions on the enhancement of immunosensor characteristics. Biotinylated antibody of anti-PSA immobilized on the surface of prepared transducer (PCys-GQDs-GNPs) has been used for detection of PSA using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and chronoamperometry (CHA) techniques. In summary, we proposed an innovative label-free electrochemical immunosensor for quantitative detection of PSA based on PCys-GQDs-GNPs as transducing materials modified gold electrode. Combining the sensitive signal enhancement ability of GQDs-GNPs and the stability of P-Cys, the resulting immunosensor displayed excellent electrochemical behavior for determination of PSA antigen. According to electrochemical properties and suitable electrical conductivity, PCys-GQDs-GNPs may be enhance electrochemical signals. So, the electron transfer efficiency could be improved. On the other hand, immobilizing amount of anti-PSA could be enhanced. Therefore, sensitivity of the sensor was improved using the proposed modifier. The proposed immunosensor showed excellent sensitivity as well as long-term stability. The immunosensor was employed for the detection of PSA in a linear concentration range of 2–9pgmL−1 with detection limit of 1.2pgmL−1.

l-Cysteine, l-Arginine, Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Tetrachloroauric acid (HAuCl4) was from Merck. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution (0.05M) was prepared by Na2HPO4 (0.1M) and NaH2PO4 (0.1M) with 0.15M of NaCl in deionized water. Anti-human prostate specific antigen (anti-PSA antibody) and PSA were purchased from CanAg Company (Gothenburg, Sweden). Human blood samples were obtained from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Research Center (Tabriz, Iran).

Electrochemical measurements were conducted using conventional three-electrode cell (from Metrohm), containing Ag/AgCl-saturated KCl as reference, a platinum wire as counter electrodes and gold (Au) working electrode (d=2mm) which powered by an electrochemical system comprising of AUTOLAB system with PGSTAT 302N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands). The system was run on a PC using NOVA 1.11 software.

The surface morphology of electrode surface was characterized by high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi-SU8020, Czech) with an operating voltage of 3kV, and chemical compositions of the GNP were analysed by EDX coupled with the FE-SEM equipment.

GQDs was synthesized and characterized by a method described in an earlier work [14]. Briefly, the simple and rapid bottom-up method was applied for preparation of GQDs solution based on citric acid pyrolysis reaction followed by the alkaline NaOH treatment of the carbonized solution.

For preparation of the Au electrode, the bare gold electrode was polished with aluminium polishing cloth for about ten minutes. Then the electrode was immersed in 1:3 water/acetone mixture and rinsed with deionized water to eliminate organic contaminants. The last step of the cleaning was performed by electropolishing the electrode in 0.5M H2SO4 solution by performing cyclic voltammetry in the range of −0.3 to 0.8V with 15 consecutive cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electropolymerization of l-Cys on the surface of Au
ce:section.ce:para: There have been several reports for the fabrication of biosensors based on electrodeposition of l-Cys on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with applications in determination of drugs [15,16] and other chemicals [17–19]. The electrochemical polymerization of l-Cys on Au electrode has been rarely reported [20]. Typically, the l-Cys modification of Au electrode is carried out by dipping the electrode in a specific solution of cysteine, allowing for spontaneous assembly of l-Cys molecules on the gold surface [21,22].

l-Cys is a prominent amino acid, famous for carrying-thiol functional group in its side chain. It is known that cysteine anchors on gold surface, via establishing strong covalent bonding. Several approaches have been carried out to consider the characteristics of the Au-S bond. As it is evident in FTIR spectra of self-assembled layers of l-Cys on Au electrode surface, the strong 2550cm−1 attributed to SH functional group of l-Cys disappeared after incubating Au electrode surface with the l-Cys solution, proving the dissociation of S–H and chemisorption of l-Cys on the gold substrate through Au–S bonding [23]. Xue et al. [26] evaluated several factors affecting the strength of Au-S covalent bound either in self-assembled monolayer or in isolated single molecule mode by AFM-based SMFS. For example according to their study, at higher pH and increased incubation time, covalent contacts overweighs coordination bonds in single isolated molecules, while the surface properties, such as cleanness and incubation time affects the monolayer formation. The oxidized gold surface via electrochemical oxidation or chemical oxidation provides more strong Au–S covalent bonds in both cases.

Aiming to develop a uniform thin film of P-Cys on the surface of Au electrode, the electropolymerization of l-Cys was implemented on the clean electrode by immersing electrode in 0.005M l-Cys/PBS solution and applying CV in the potential range of −1 to 1V vs. Ag/AgCl for 5 cycles (See Fig. 1 ). The Cysteine-coated Au electrode was rinsed with deionized water and prepared for the next modification step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of GQDs on the surface of P-Cys modified Au electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The graphene-based materials, such as graphene oxide, reduced-graphene oxide and graphene sheets, etc., have been widely incorporated in PSA electrochemical biosensors for designing various sensing schemes. These materials have proved capability of accepting different roles (electrode surface modification, signal tag, etc.) owing to their unique mechanical properties, excellent electrical conductivity and catalytic behaviors [23–27]. GQD, the new member of the graphene-based materials, combines all the unique features of these materials with nano features, which makes it highly preferential for modifying electrochemical sensing platform [5]. It is predicted that the free amine groups of the previously coated P-Cys layer, interacts with the oxygen bearing functional groups of GQDs, establishing highly powerful chemical bonding via amidation process, which helps stabilizing the electrodeposited GQDs layer [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of GNPs on the surface of P-Cys-GQDs modified Au electrode using soft template method
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have revealed dependency of deposition potential on the size and morphology of generated GNPs. Shi et al. demonstrated that the crystal density of generated GNPs increases by shifting toward negative electrodeposition potentials from −0.3 to 0.3V on ITO electrode, because of increase in nucleation rate when oversaturation occurs at higher overpotentials [29]. The reduction of AuCl4 − on Au electrode initiates at 0.15V, leading to full nucleation at E=0.20, therefore, the potential was adjusted on E=0 where no reaction occurs [30]. For electrodeposition of gold nanoseeds on the surface of Au electrode pre-modified by P-Cys-GQDs, the electrodeposition of metallic gold ions by chronoamperometry technique was carried out in the cell containing triple solution of 20mM HAuCl4, 500mM H2SO4 and 50mM Arginine. The electrodeposition potential was set at E=0.0V and time duration of the chronoamperometry was adjusted at 600s (Fig. 3 ). For the fabrication mechanism of GNPs, it can be mentioned that gold atoms or clusters are initially and rapidly formed, followed by promoted nucleation. The arginine adsorption process causes to formation of a GNPS-stable arginine layer in which arginine is involved to the surface via the zwitterions functional group, and its positively charged guanidinium functional group tends outward into the solution. Therefore, AuCl4 − ions was electrostatically absorb on the surface of P-Cys and cause to preferential growth of Au by the diffusion-limited aggregation mechanism.
In summary, Scheme 1 has explained the fabrication procedure of the electrochemical immunosensor.

After preparing the substrate containing P-Cys, GQDs and GNPs, the electrode was gently rinsed and after drying, 3mL of the anti-PSA was casted carefully on the electrode surface. The antibody-coated modified electrode tip was covered with a cap to increase moisture and was kept at 4°C for 2h. Later, the electrode was rinsed with deionized water to remove excess antibody, followed by immersing the electrode, Au/P-Cys-GQDs-GNPs-Anti-PSA, in bovine serum albumin (BSA) solution for 5min to block active sites in order to avoid nonspecific adsorption of the antigen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The SEM and EDX characterization of the modified sensing interface
ce:section.ce:para: SEM imaging was applied to evaluate the morphology of the electrodeposited layers. The SEM images of the electrode surfaces related to Au/P-Cys, Au/P-Cys/GQDs and Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs are represented in Fig. 4. The SEM images reveal scattered particles of l-Cys in broad size range with relatively low coverage (Fig. 4 A), the evenly deposited, and closely packed GQDs layer in approximate size range of 30nm (Fig. 4 B) and the uniform GNPs layer with particles of similar non-spherical morphology related to Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs (Fig. 4C). The EDX characterization technique was employed to further study the formation of P-Cys-GQDs and P-Cys-GQDs-GNPs layers on Au electrode (Fig. 4).

As evident in Fig. 5 (a), signals for C and O elements belong to both cysteine and GQDs layers, while N is only attributed to P-Cys layer, as cysteine include amine groups. Si and Al elements are probably due to the presence of some impurities in the solid electrode. The large and sharp Au peak is the typical signal for bulk gold element. The corresponding EDX spectra of Au/P-Cys-GQDs-GNPs (Fig. 5(b)) demonstrates the increase in Au peak, which clearly indicates the electrodeposition of nano-size gold layer.

By comparing the CV profiles in the applied potential range of −1 to 1V in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.4) for the different modifications of Au electrode, namely bare Au electrode, Au/P-Cys-anti-PSA, Au/P-Cys/GQDs/anti-PSA and Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs/anti-PSA, the remarkable increase in cathodic peak of the gold around 0.3V and the appearance of the symmetric anodic peak at 0.9 is observable. These prove the successful electrodeposition of gold nanostructures on the Au electrode surface (Fig. 6 ).

It has been previously reported that upon immobilizing antibodies onto the electrode surface, the net charge of the antibody will determine the surface charge of the electrode and when antigen molecules are present in the solution, the recognition immunoreaction will occur, which can alter the surface charge of the electrode [25]. Therefore, four types of modified electrodes were evaluated for their electrochemical behavior in blank solution and the solution containing 10cc PBS and 3mL PSA. As can be seen in three relevant CV voltammograms, in the presence of antigen, two peaks in the 0.5–1 potential region probably related to the oxidation of l-cysteine disappears because of the charge alteration that the antigen/antibody recognition event poses on the electrode surface (6b). The next considerable change upon recognition of antigen by antibody is evident in the figure (6c), where oxidation current peak related to the functional groups on GQDs layer in 0.89 potential disappears. The last remarkable change is the emergence of a new oxidation peak in the voltammogram of the forth electrode in −0.5 vs. Ag/AgCl region after adding antigen, which has implication for determination of analytical region.

The comparison of CVs in the absence and presence of PSA with different modified electrodes demonste a new anodic peak at −0.45V on the surface of Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs/anti-PSA. The larger lowering of the overvoltage (−0.45V) detected in the presence of PSA obviously indicates the crucial role of P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs for detection of PSA antigen. These results confirmed that prepared nanocomposite could accelerate the rate of electron transfer which is necessary for redox reaction. Therefore, P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs is a suitable mediator to shuttle electron between PSA and working electrode. This observation is also linked to the high conductivity and inherent ability of P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs. In addition, that might be related to the excellent properties of P-Cys as conductive matrix and GQDs/GNPs as dual signal amplification elements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic behaviors of the PSA immunosensors
ce:section.ce:para: Kinetic behavior of the proposed immunosensor was evaluated by performing CV in different scan rates from 0.02 to 1V/s. As evident in the CV plot (Fig. 7 A), the width of the voltammogram widened gradually and the three dominant peaks, two anodic and one cathodic peak raised considerably by increase in scan rate. It can be concluded that in the slower scan rates, the electron exchange occur slower at the electrode surface to record peak currents leading to narrower voltammogram with shorter current peaks. Accordingly, the second oxidation peak at ∼0.6V emerges only after increasing scan rate from initial 0.02 to 0.075 proving the slow electron transfer mechanism.

The scan rate analysis revealed the direct dependence of the peak current on the selected range of sweep rates. As evidence in Fig 7B, the slopes for the relationship of current-potential to the first anodic peak and cathodic peaks are steeper compared to the second anodic peak. In order to investigate the nature of the electrode reaction, dependency of the peak current versus the square root of the scan rate (v 1/2) and Napierian logarithm of peak current versus the Napierian logarithm of the sweep rate (ln v) for the peaks were plotted [31]. The linear dependency for Ip-v 1/2 and LnIp vs. Ln v, suggesting the diffusion controlled nature of the three electrochemical reactions (Fig. 7B & C). Therefore, it can be claimed that the electrochemical processes followed the Randles-Sevcik equation, specific for diffusion controlled electrochemical reactions [32]:
(1)
Ip =2.69×105 AD1/2 n 3/2 v 1/2 C
where Ip is the peak current (mA), A stands for the electroactive surface area (cm2), D attributes to the diffusion coefficient of the molecule in solution (cm2 s−1), n is the number of transferred electrons, v is the scan rate (Vs−1) and C corresponds to the bulk concentration of the analyte (molcm−3).

Also, the redox peak currents increased proportionally by the sweep rate indicating that sensor system has electro-catalytic property with irreversible nature. On the other hand, to elucidate the mechanism of mass transfer, two styles were used. I), Investigation on the variation of Ip versus v 1/2 (Figs. 7B). Using this approach, the intercept of the Ip versus v 1/2 was found to be 10−5, which indicates that the mass transfer was controlled by diffusion mechanism [33]. II) Investigation on the variation of lnIp versus lnv. The results were illustrated in Fig. 7C. The slopes of the plot in Fig. 7C for oxidation and reduction peaks were found as 0.6133 and 0.6874, respectively. If the slope of lnIp versus lnv is close to 0.5, the process is diffusion controlled. Also, if the slope is close to 1, the process is controlled through adsorption [33]. As, the slope was calculated about 0.6, it can be concluded that the mass transfer is a diffusion controlled process which is in agreement with the results of the first approach.

In addition, the mass transfer mechanism can also be evaluated by electron transfer coefficient, a, of Eq. (2) [33].
(2)
E p =
(
R T
2 α F
)
ln
v + c o n s t a n t
where Ep is electrode potential, R is gas constant (8.314, JK−1 mol−1), T is temperature in Kelvin scale (298K), F is Faraday’s constant (96486, Asmol−1), and u is potential sweep rate (Vs−1). If the value of a is close to 0.5, the system is a diffusion controlled [34]. Using this equation, the a value was obtained as 0.33 which further supports that the mass transfer of the system is diffusion controlled. In Fig. 7D, the plot of Eq. (2) obtained from lnIp versus Ep (Tafel plot) have been illustrated.

The special surface coverage (Г* ) was calculated via the slope of the redox peak currents (A) versus sweep rate (V/s) using Eq. (3):
(3)
I p =
(
n
2
F
2
4 R T
)
v
A Γ
*
where A is total electrode surface(cm2), and Г* (molcm−2) is special surface covered with Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs. Based on the obtained results, the slope of redox peak currents versus sweep rates (Fig. 7E) was 0.0002 and the Г* value was calculated as 7.11×10−9 molcm−2. When Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs was used as the electrode modifier, the Г* value was improved. Therefore, by using Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs as electrode modifier, it is possible to increase the efficiency of the prepared immunosensor. This improved efficiency could be a result of modifier and lead to also an increase in the conductivity.

The standard PSA samples in the PBS solution, as detection medium, were used to evaluate the sensitivity of the prepared PSA immunosensor by employing chronoamperometry technique. As the followed protocol was performed without incorporating redox pair and mediation, first, the potential analytical range for PSA sample was investigated considering the behaviors of electrodes with different modifications in −1 to 1.2V potential range (Fig. 8 A). In the next stage, the analytical potential area with the least interferences in the CV plots was evaluated and considering the forth modification of the Au electrode (Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs/anti-PSA), −0.5 to 0V was spotted as the desired analytical potential range. Finally, chronoamperometry was applied in the fixed value of −0.3V for tracing the antibody-antigen immunoreaction, which exhibited orderly decline in current upon decrease in the PSA concentration. The low concentration of 1.8pgmL−1 PSA could be determined in the linear potential range of 2–9pgmL−1 (Fig. 8B).
The results exhibited that this method performed with a higher sensitivity when it was compared to other conventional detection approaches [41–47] (Table 1 ).
Also, Table 2 compare the results of this label free biosensor with the previously reported immunosensor for detection of PSA.

According to the obtained results, it was possible to apply this technique to the quantitative analysis of PSA in real samples. As mentioned previously, the PBS of pH 7.4 was selected as the supporting electrolyte for the quantification of PSA. For this purpose, 0.5mL of whole blood were taken and added into supporting electrolytes to reach a total volume of 5mL.

Using chronoamperometry technique, the resulting sample solutions were measured using the prepared immunosensor. The corresponding analytical results displayed in Table 3 . Excellent recoveries varying from 98.3% to102.1% were obtained, which illustrates that the established method are quite desirable and qualified for practical sample testing.

We have developed an ultrasensitive electrochemical immunosensing for PSA detection based on P-Cys as conductive polymeric matrix and GQDs/GNPs as a synergetic signal amplification element. For the first time, fully electrochemical methodology was used to prepare a new transducer on a gold surface which provided a high surface area to immobilize a high surface concentration of the anti-PSA. Also, GNPs were electrodeposited on the surface of P-Cys-GQDs with the assistance of an amino acid as a soft template. Therefore, a green electrodeposition method was developed for the preparation of a new immunosensor. Experimental results show that, proposed immunoassay can effectively improve electron transfer efficiency based on synergistic effect of P-Cys as conductive matrix and GQDs/GNPs as dual signal amplification elements. It was found that, the new morphology of GNPs synthesized in the presence of soft template facilitated the electron transfer. Also, P-Cys and GQDs provided a biocompatible environment with a large specific surface area for the further immobilization of anti-PSA. The immunosensor was employed for the detection of PSA in physiological pH. Under optimized condition the calibration curve for PSA concentration was linear up to 2–9pgmL−1 with low limit of quantification 1.8pgmL−1. Therefore, proposed immunosensor supply valuable information for early clinical and point-of-care diagnosis of prostate cancer as a simple, rapid and fully automated assay. Hopefully, the immobilized strategy and the detection method can be further developed for other cancer biomarkers.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by the Ministry of Health and Medical Education of Iran (The Grant for Young assistant Professors), Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, for financial and instrumental supporting of this research.
